behaviors.description: Error message names the missing required key by its exact config path when absent from both file and env
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is entirely within error-message construction logic — key lookup in a parsed dict, env var scan, and string formatting. No module boundary handoff is needed to trigger this. REAL-INFRA: The YAML file can be represented as a pre-parsed dict; env vars can be monkeypatched via os.environ; no actual file I/O is needed. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking env lookup would hide nothing here since the point is the error string content, not side effects. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected — the failure appears inside a single validation function, not at a handoff between modules.
behaviors.test_description: Patch os.environ to have no TOOL_* variables, pass a pre-parsed config dict missing 'database.host', invoke the validation function, and assert the raised exception message contains the string 'database.host'.

behaviors.description: Loader resolves config file path relative to XDG_CONFIG_HOME when that env var is set
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The behavior is about path computation logic — a single expression that conditionally substitutes XDG_CONFIG_HOME for ~/.config. This is one function's algorithm. REAL-INFRA: No real filesystem access is needed to verify the returned path string; monkeypatching XDG_CONFIG_HOME and calling the path-resolution function is sufficient. MOCK-HIDE: The only risk of a missed failure would be if 'resolves' is interpreted as 'successfully opens the file at that path', which would require Integration. The behavior text says 'resolves the config file path', not 'reads from that path'. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered for the end-to-end open; rejected because the stated behavior is path resolution, not file reading.
behaviors.test_description: Monkeypatch os.environ with XDG_CONFIG_HOME='/tmp/custom', call the config path resolver, and assert the returned path starts with '/tmp/custom/tool/config.yaml'.

behaviors.description: Environment variable with TOOL_ prefix overrides the corresponding key from the config file
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the merge/precedence logic: iterate env vars matching the prefix, map them to config keys, overwrite file values. This is a single function's algorithm — no module boundary handoff required. REAL-INFRA: The file source can be a pre-parsed dict; env vars monkeypatched; no I/O needed. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing is hidden by using a dict instead of a real file — the override logic operates on already-parsed data. REJECTION-GATE: Integration considered if the mapping from env var name to nested key path is done by a separate module, but the behavior's own failure mode is the override rule itself, not the boundary between modules.
behaviors.test_description: Monkeypatch TOOL_DATABASE__HOST='envhost', pass a config dict with database.host='filehost', call the merge function, and assert the result has database.host == 'envhost'.

behaviors.description: Error message includes the line number when the config file contains a YAML syntax error
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in exception-handling code that wraps the YAML parser call and extracts the line number from the parser's exception. This is one function boundary. REAL-INFRA: YAML parsing is in-process; a malformed YAML string can be passed directly as input — no real file I/O needed. The pyyaml/ruamel exception carries the line number without any filesystem involvement. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the YAML parser would hide the real exception structure; the test must call the real parser with a real syntax-error string. REJECTION-GATE: Integration considered if the loader reads from an actual file path (since file I/O wraps the parse call), but the behavior's failure is in exception message construction, verifiable by passing a bad YAML string to the parse-and-load function directly.
behaviors.test_description: Pass a YAML string with a deliberate syntax error on line 3 (e.g., a bad indentation or invalid character) to the config parser function, catch the raised ConfigError, and assert the message contains '3' or 'line 3'.

behaviors.description: Loader creates the config directory and writes a defaults file when the directory does not exist at startup
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure involves multiple operations in sequence — existence check, os.makedirs, open/write, then continuing with the defaults. These span the OS boundary and require real state to verify. REAL-INFRA: 'Writes a defaults file' is explicit file I/O whose correctness (directory actually exists, file has correct content, permissions are correct) can only be verified against a real filesystem. An in-process fake (mocking os.makedirs + open) would verify calls were made but would not catch bugs where the path is wrong, the file content is malformed, or makedirs raises unexpectedly. A real tmpdir (pytest tmp_path) surfaces all of these. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the filesystem ops would allow the test to pass even if the written file has wrong content or the path is subtly wrong. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered but rejected — this is local filesystem I/O, no subprocess or real network involved. The real tmpdir provided by pytest is sufficient to catch all realistic failure modes.
behaviors.test_description: Using pytest's tmp_path fixture, monkeypatch the config base path to a nonexistent subdirectory inside tmp_path, invoke the startup loader, then assert the directory exists, the defaults file exists at the expected path, and the file parses as valid YAML with expected default keys.
